data.title: Best fixed-rate bonds: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
Fixed-rate bonds are savings accounts that lock away your funds for a set period of time, paying a guaranteed rate of interest throughout this term.

They can be a shrewd move in times when savings rates are on the way down – as they are now – since the rate you fix at will be unaffected until the term ends.

However, you’ll need to be sure you won’t need the cash in the meantime. Many accounts simply won’t let you access your savings until the term is over, while those that do allow early withdrawals will usually deduct your interest as a penalty.

This guide will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best fixed-rate bonds in April
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond
  • Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds
  • Fixed-rate bond FAQs

What are fixed-rate bonds?

Fixed-rate bonds may also be referred to as fixed-rate savings accounts, or even fixed-term deposits – depending on the provider. In essence, they are savings accounts that pay a set amount of interest, and last for a set amount of time.

They commonly span from nine months to five years and, in theory, the longer the term you commit to, the higher the interest rate you receive.

However, this is not always the case – during times when rates are expected to fall over the longer term, savings providers will increase rates for shorter-term bonds in a bid to avoid paying over the odds in future.

You will often need to pay in a lump sum at the start of the term, rather than making regular deposits, so these accounts tend to suit those with larger amounts to save.

The interest can be paid annually, quarterly or monthly, depending on your provider. In some cases, interest may only be paid at the end of the term – if you have a large amount saved over a number of years, receiving all the interest in one go could mean you will exceed your personal savings allowance and have to pay tax on your returns.

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update every day.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best fixed-rate bonds in April

Use the table below to explore your options for a fixed-rate deal.

For the top rates where you can access your cash whenever you like, see our guide to the best easy access accounts and the best current accounts.

Alternatively, our guide to the best notice accounts can show you the top accounts that require you to wait a little while before your money is released.

While you’re saving, you might be keen to shield your money from a potential tax bill – our guide to the best cash Isas can help you find a tax-efficient account.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond

Rachel Springall, of the analyst Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Picking the right fixed bond term entirely depends on someone’s circumstances and how soon they wish to access their pot. There are bonds with fixed terms of just a few months, so these might be useful for someone who has a big lump sum but may need to use it before the year is over, such as a deposit for a home.

“There are fixed bonds available that pay interest monthly, which may be suited to customers looking to supplement their income.”

She also warned customers to consider the personal savings allowance (PSA) when it came to accounts that were not Isas, as there could be tax to pay on the interest you earn.

Fixed-rate bonds are worth considering for anyone who wants certainty in their returns – this can be particularly handy in times of volatility. 

While it may be tempting to stick to the high street brands you’re familiar with, it’s not always the route to the best accounts.

“As it stands, some of the top rates available are from challenger banks, which should not be overlooked,” Ms Springall added.

Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds

While Premium Bonds and fixed-rate bonds might sound similar, they are very different:

  • Premium Bonds are only provided by National Savings & Investments (NS&I), while fixed-rate bonds are offered by a number of savings providers.
  • Premium Bonds aren’t savings accounts; you don’t get paid interest. Instead, each £1 bond is entered into a monthly prize draw, where you could win between £25-£1m. Fixed-rate bonds pay a set amount of interest for the duration of the term.
  • Premium Bonds prizes are paid tax-free; savings interest from fixed-rate bonds is taxable unless it’s a fixed-rate cash Isa.

Fixed-rate bond FAQS

You might have to pay tax on the interest your fixed-rate bond earns if it exceeds your tax-free allowance, unlike the money stored in an Isa.

This allowance depends on your tax bracket; basic-rate taxpayers can earn £1,000 in interest tax-free but the limit is £500 for higher-rate taxpayers and no personal savings allowance for additional-rate taxpayers.

Those with incomes of less than £17,570 may be able to benefit from the additional starting rate for savings, which gives those on lower incomes up to £5,000 as an extra tax-free savings allowance.

If you usually pay tax by self-assessment, you will be asked to detail the amount of savings interest you have earned over the tax year. If you pay tax via Pay As You Earn (PAYE), any tax on savings interest is usually calculated automatically and your tax code may be adjusted if you have tax to pay.

You must submit a tax return if you earn more than £10,000 in savings interest – regardless of whether or not you usually file one.

This will depend on what rates are available at the time you are looking for a bond, as well as whether the account suits your circumstances.

The best way to determine how “good” an interest rate is, is to see how it compares with other similar types of bond on the market. If it can easily be beaten by other providers, it might be worth looking elsewhere.

Our table of the best fixed-rate bonds above shows some of the top deals currently available.

NS&I’s Premium Bonds are not fixed-rate bonds – and they work slightly differently to other types of savings accounts.

They do not pay any interest, instead every £1 you have invested is assigned its own Premium Bonds number, which is entered into a monthly prize draw, whether you could win between £25 and £1m. Our guide to Premium Bonds explains how they work in more detail.

NS&I does offer fixed-rate bonds, including Guaranteed Growth Bonds and Guaranteed Income Bonds – see our guide to NS&I savings accounts to find out more.

data.title: Building in London is becoming impossible, warns housing giant
data.description:
Building homes in London is becoming impossible because of high taxes and red tape, a major housebuilder has warned.

Berkeley Group said on Wednesday that it was halting new land purchases and reducing investment in its building projects, most of which are in the capital or other urban areas.

The company said the conflict in Iran, which is pushing up costs, and a deteriorating economic outlook contributed to its decision to pull back from the market.

However, Rob Perrins, the chief executive of Berkeley, criticised red tape and taxes that he said risked making London effectively uninvestable.

Berkeley said land prices were becoming “overheated” because it was too easy for other businesses to bid for land. Mr Perrins said that Big Yellow Group, the self-storage giant, is his “biggest competitor at the moment”.

“The Government needs to prioritise housebuilding land and not allow Big Yellow to buy all the land, because London will have no [new] homes, fundamentally, if we carry on as we are,” he said.

“You can’t keep increasing taxation on fewer and fewer houses. We have three types of taxes that are too high – development tax, customer-facing tax and corporate tax, which other land uses don’t get.”

In its update to the market, Berkeley criticised “an unprecedented increase in cost and regulation, at a time of increasing interest rates and faltering consumer confidence, amidst prolonged geopolitical and macro-economic volatility and uncertainty”.

Mr Perrins criticised the building safety levy, a tax aimed at funding cladding remediation work and the community infrastructure levy, a charge that councils use to fund infrastructure such as schools and health services.

He said: “The building safety levy should be dropped. We already pay a residential property developer tax of 4pc. Our corporation tax is [effectively] 29pc. We’ve got all the employee taxes.

“There’s a huge amount of what I call customer-facing taxes [such as] stamp duty, and then there’s a huge amount of development tax.

“The Government is not focusing and prioritising affordable housing, [but] focusing on tax-raising measures like the Community Infrastructure Levy.”

Building safety planning rules had added around a year to the time taken to gain planning approval and begin construction on projects, Berkeley said.

Mr Perrins said the quagmire in London meant Labour’s manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament was looking increasingly far-fetched.

Underpinning that ambition is the Mayor of London’s target to build 88,000 homes a year in the capital.

Mr Perrins said: “If London has a requirement for 88,000 homes, and we’re building 10pc of those, it probably sums up how difficult it is for the Government to hit its target. It just puts it in complete context.”

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary and Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, unveiled an emergency package to boost housebuilding in the capital in October. Reforms include cutting the proportion of affordable homes required for a site from 35pc to 20pc.

Mr Perrins said the temporary measures were to be “applauded”, but warned their success would depend entirely on how “forward-thinking” each borough is.

“My worry is, if boroughs don’t want to follow the messaging, it will not change behaviours on the ground,” he said.

“From a policy point of view, I can’t fault [the measures] … but if local authorities won’t implement them, then it will not translate into new houses quickly.”

He pointed to recent efforts to build 586 homes in Kingston, which were refused by the council after five years of negotiations.

Mr Perrins said the Government needed to consider more measures, such as reducing taxes and regulation on builders, and bringing in demand stimulus through Help to Buy-style schemes for buyers and stamp duty reductions.

Shares in Berkeley plummeted by nearly 18pc on Wednesday morning, marking the steepest fall in a decade. The stock has fallen 24pc over the last month.

The housebuilder cut its profit forecasts following the update. It now expects to generate £1.4bn of pre-tax profit in the four years between 2027 and 2030, roughly equivalent to £350m a year.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said the new forecast was around a third lower than previous estimates.

Berkeley said it would redirect its focus to developing its existing land holdings and buying plots through joint-venture agreements, rather than on its own.

Its existing land holdings consist of sites for more than 50,000 homes, with a further pipeline of more than 10,000 in London and the South East.

Aynsley Lammin of Investec said London was the “most difficult area” in the UK for housebuilding and said Berkeley’s decisions were “reflective of the difficult situations” builders face in the capital.

“The affordability constraints are greater. If you’re a first-time buyer, just the hurdle to raise a deposit to get the mortgage is that much more difficult in London,” he said.

Data published by Nationwide in January showed the average first-time buyer must put down a deposit of £44,800 for a home, well above the national average of £23,000.

Mr Lammin said: “Investor appetite has waned as well, particularly from overseas investors, with all of the regulations on investment properties, buy-to-let - that’s been a big factor for Berkeley Group. London was more of an investor market and that’s become very difficult.

“Developers also face more regulatory and cost burdens in London… Some of it is obviously economy-wide, but it’s concentrated and magnified in London. Everything just becomes that much more difficult.”

data.title: Tudor manor refurb plan branded ‘poor taxidermy’
data.description:
Plans to redevelop a Tudor manor house have been criticised as “poor taxidermy” because the interior is expected to be completely rebuilt.

The derelict Winstanley Hall, near Wigan, which dates back to around 1573, has been empty since previous regeneration plans fell through 40 years ago.

However, new plans to renovate the site and turn the building into 36 apartments and houses have been criticised by neighbours.

Syd Cottle, from the Save Winstanley Greenbelt campaign, said: “Only the facade will be retained, so what you’ll have is a brand new building replacing the internal heritage aspects of the structure.

“I compare it to the building equivalent of poor taxidermy.”

Winstanley Hall was owned for 400 years by the Bankes, a successful coal-mining family during the 18th and early 19th centuries. The house was last occupied in the mid-1980s.

Since then, the structure has deteriorated significantly from a lack of maintenance and slates being stolen from the roof, which led to water damage and the collapse of the building’s interior.

A spokesman from Historic England said: “We are pleased to finally see a proposal for what remains of the house and outbuildings to be retained and converted to new homes by a developer.

“However, we remain concerned that the site has been allowed to deteriorate for so many years to its current condition – despite its high national listed status.”

According to Historic England, Grade II* buildings are particularly important buildings of more than special interest. Around 5.8 per cent of listed buildings are Grade II*.

Last year, Kingswood Homes bought the site and has since submitted plans to Wigan council to redevelop it and create nearly 400 new homes.

Locals have objected to the plans, and a petition regarding the proposal has received more than 1,000 signatures.

Deborah Orr, from the Save Winstanley Greenbelt campaign, said there seemed to be a “build, baby, build ethos”, which “feels like it is not properly thought out”.

She said: “We don’t want them to build loads of houses that are not going to be sustainable, will cause flooding, traffic, overcrowding and put pressure on our services.

“The area already floods a lot. Where is the analysis of adding a concrete jungle on that?”

Hayley Pierce, the Green Party candidate for Standish ward in Wigan, said the plans would see the old hall “gutted”.

She added: “The planned proposal for development on the site on and around Winstanley Hall is not a restoration that would bring the hall into use for the community, it is a plan to gut the old hall and fill it with new build flats.” 

‘Last chance of being saved’

Paul Jones, the managing director of Kingswood Homes, said: “We are trying to save the hall and bring forward social housing as we are in a time where there’s a massive housing shortage.

“We want to restore the hall and save it, and it is its last chance of being saved.

“People can claim that it’s not a restoration, but Historic England has said it is a restoration and it is supportive of it.

“All those concerns will be assessed by the council as part of the application. If there is an impact, then it has to be mitigated.”

Wigan council has been approached for comment.

data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: Energy bills bailout ‘could pay for itself’, says City
data.description:
An energy bailout for millions of households would pay for itself, economists have said.

Deutsche Bank said a support package worth £4bn would ease inflation by holding down gas and electricity bills.

Economists said this would result in lower interest rates, which in turn would save the Government billions of pounds in reduced borrowing costs.

The intervention is likely to raise pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, after she has so far resisted calls to roll out a wide-ranging energy-bills support package after criticising the cost of the Tories’ bailout in the wake of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

However, Deutsche Bank’s Sanjay Raja said any potential bailout could “pay for itself”.

“Fiscal support that would actively reduce CPI inflation could end up paying for itself – even adding to the fiscal headroom,” he said.

It comes as EDF and E.On Next, two of Britain’s biggest energy suppliers, warned that household gas and electricity bills could top £2,000 by autumn because of the war in Iran.

EDF suggests annualised bills will rise to £2,047 by October, up from its forecasts of £1,937 for July. E.On Next has similar figures, predicting £1,955 from July and £2,064 from October.

EDF also warned that prices could rise even further by October.

It said: “Our forecasts from July 2026 onwards have continued to rise week-on-week, driven by increases in wholesale prices arising from ongoing strikes in Iran and the Middle East.”

EDF and Eon collectively supply around seven million households in the UK.

The expected increase in bills is a direct consequence of the conflict in the Middle East, which has sent oil and gas prices soaring.

As well as higher energy bills, households have also been hit by soaring petrol and diesel prices.

Petrol now costs more than £1.50 per litre, up from below £1.33 at the end of February, according to the RAC. Diesel is up more than 35p at nearly £1.78 on average.

This has prompted calls for Ms Reeves to postpone the rise in fuel duty scheduled to take effect in September.

If the Chancellor freezes fuel duty and cuts £150 from household bills, this would ultimately cost the Government £4bn, said Mr Raja.

But it would also trim 0.4 percentage points off inflation. His calculations indicate this could be enough to take around 0.3 percentage points off interest rates in financial markets.

Combined with the boost to growth that would come from avoiding the blow from higher rates, Mr Raja suggests that the Government would have to borrow £5bn less than expected.

“In effect, paying for the entirety of fiscal support levied to reduce consumer price inflation,” he said.

The idea of government support lowering borrowing costs has also been raised by Zack Polanski.

The Green Party leader said this month that “the Government should guarantee right now that it will not allow energy bills to rise beyond the April-June price cap”, proposing an £8.4bn scheme.

He said: “If the price cap rises, we will see interest rates rise, mortgage rates up, bond yields up and inflation up.

“And we will be back into the doom loop that has done untold damage to our economy and caused misery for households across the UK for years now.”

Britain is particularly exposed to inflation from global markets because it is a net importer of energy.

Economists expect Britain to face a jump in inflation, which is potentially twice as big as that facing the eurozone.

A survey by the National Institute of Economic and Social Research found the average economist expected an increase in inflation of 0.8 to 1.1 percentage points in the UK, compared with 0.4 to 0.7 in the eurozone.

data.title: Marie Claire publisher loses a third of its value after warning of AI hit
data.description:
The publisher behind Marie Claire and Country Life lost a third of its value after warning AI search tools were hitting reader numbers.

Shares in Future, which also owns The Week and price comparison site Go.Compare, crashed by 30pc on Tuesday after it admitted a decline in traffic from Google had been “more pronounced than anticipated”.

Publishers have been left reeling from Google’s rollout of AI overviews at the top of its search results. These are designed to answer search queries using information scraped from websites and have been blamed for sharp drops in the number of people clicking through to sites.

Future said the change had led to a fall in readership, denting its advertising and e-commerce revenues and driving up the cost of ad spots across the industry.

The company warned that revenue would decline by a low single-digit percentage in the second half of the year. This could mean a fall of anywhere between 1pc and 5pc.

The share slump wiped almost £100m off the company’s market value. Future has suffered a collapse over the past five years, with the company’s value down 90pc since 2021 as shares fell from nearly £40 each to just £2.75 today.

According to the Reuters Institute, traffic from Google is down by a third to news sites because of the AI change and news outlets expect it to plunge a further 43pc over the next three years.

The Competition and Markets Authority said in January that Google will have to let publishers decide whether they appear in AI summaries after the News Media Association – which represents titles including The Times, The Guardian and The Telegraph – accused the tech giant of “exploiting” its dominant position in search.

Google will also be banned from “manipulating” its search results by retaliating against companies that criticise it or favouring businesses that spend heavily on Google advertising. Google denies engaging in such practices.

Nevertheless, the rollout of the new technology is still hurting free-to-read websites reliant on digital advertising.

Reach, which publishes The Mirror and Daily Express as well as dozens of regional titles, suffered its biggest loss in more than a decade last year following a drop in reader numbers.

Future has launched an initiative called “Google Zero”, aimed at reducing its reliance on search referrals for revenue.

The publisher is instead seeking to make more money directly from its readers and is offering new services such as helping brands become more visible on AI services such as ChatGPT.

Future said its takeover of SheerLuxe, a social media-based beauty and fashion brand, was progressing well and that price comparison site Go.Compare had returned to growth.

The Future board said it believed the company was fundamentally undervalued and would explore options for brands that were not delivering value for shareholders.

Kevin Li Ying, the chief executive, said: “Whilst we are disappointed with the impact of the changes in the search ecosystem on our near-term trading performance, we are making good progress in executing the elements of our growth strategy that are in our control.

“This includes a laser focus on driving the platform effect to optimise monetisation across our brands through our Google Zero strategy and leveraging AI as a new source of revenue through products like Future Optic.

“The board remains determined to drive a return to growth and to unlock the substantial value from our unique portfolio of assets.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Times have changed, but Ed Miliband is stranded in another age
data.description:
When the Climate Change Act sailed through Parliament in 2008, it did so with overwhelming political support and barely a murmur of opposition. Only five Tory MPs voted against it on the second reading.

Such was the political consensus around the so-called “climate change emergency” that no vote at all was held when the Act was later beefed up further to make “net zero” by 2050 a legally binding commitment.

The costs were barely considered; it was 2019, and we were at the height of Greta Thunberg mania, a collective, mass panic about climate change, fuelled by the protests of a 16-year-old Swedish schoolgirl.

The then prime minister, Theresa May, wanted to be remembered for something other than her bungled handling of Brexit, and this was to be her legacy – making the UK the first major country to pass a law requiring complete decarbonisation of the economy by a defined future date.

Seven years and two energy crises later, and what was thought of as a masterstroke of global leadership back then is proving a debilitating poisoned chalice that acts as a warning to all who might follow.

Everyone can see that it is not working as hoped, and yet we remain lashed to the mast as if no longer in control of our own destiny.

Sir Keir Starmer was at his legalistic and process-driven worst last week when confronted by these issues at Prime Minister’s Questions.

Decisions on whether the UK should further develop its North Sea oil and gas reserves were not his to make, he in essence said. Rather, they were a “quasi-judicial” matter belonging to the Energy Secretary, Ed Miliband.

Here was proof positive that Miliband, already credited with having forced Sir Keir to distance himself from Trump’s war with Iran, is the real power behind the throne. Seemingly, Sir Keir cannot even make up his own mind on energy policy.

Some suggest a devious cunning in Sir Keir’s apparent impotence; that he is making Miliband wholly own a policy that is looking less and less defensible by the day.

When the moment comes, Miliband – still a popular figure among Labour members and therefore a potent threat to Sir Keir’s leadership – can be made to take the blame.

But I doubt that fully explains the Prime Minister’s refusal to take responsibility. As we know, Sir Keir is a stickler for legal process, and net zero is positively crawling with legislative and judicial determinations that collectively amount to an almost insurmountable barrier to change.

Even if outlawing further North Sea oil and gas development were not a Labour Party manifesto commitment, what can and cannot be done is closely guarded by quangos with semi-independent authority – in particular, the North Sea Transition Authority (NSTA) and the Offshore Petroleum Regulator for Environment and Decommissioning (OPRED).

Ultimate responsibility lies with the Secretary of State, but he’s hardly likely to go against judgments with which he wholeheartedly agrees. They provide useful cover.

As does the legislation, which requires the Government to set regular carbon budgets based on mandatory advice from another independent quango, the Climate Change Committee. Running down the North Sea is very much part of the plan for getting to net zero.

This objective is further buttressed by a number of judicial decisions which have required environmental impact assessments to take into account the supposed harms of actually burning the oil and gas alongside the carbon costs of extracting it.

The real game-changer was a Supreme Court judgment regarding a tiny little onshore oil prospect at Horse Hill in Surrey. Planning permission was ruled unlawful because the promoters had failed to consider the greenhouse gas emissions that would occur when the oil was ultimately burned – so-called Scope 3 emissions.

Last year, Scotland’s Court of Session used the same logic to block approval for the Jackdaw gas field and Rosebank oil prospect in the North Sea.

Fossil fuel development has become a legal quagmire worthy of Bleak House, the Charles Dickens novel that satirised the paralysis of England’s early 19th century courts system.

Indeed, it might be seen as a metaphor for the wider problem of getting anything done in today’s Britain.

The whole economy is seemingly sinking beneath a sea of lawyers, taxmen, computer-says-no government bureaucrats and environmental do-gooders.

When the boss of a company as synonymous with high corporate standards as Marks & Spencer starts yelling blue murder at rocketing energy costs, it is surely time to finally sit up and take notice.

The situation is obviously being greatly worsened by renewed conflict in the Middle East, but as M&S’s Stuart Machin points out, that’s not the root of the problem. Rather it is the costs that are loaded on top by government taxes, levies and instruction.

The Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, removed some of these levies from household bills in her Spring Statement and absorbed them onto general taxation instead.

But she did not extend the same courtesy to the business community, which she continues to treat like a milch cow.

In May 2024, just before the last general election, 120 business leaders signed an open letter backing Labour’s economic plans, saying it was “time for a change”. Not many of them will hold those sentiments today. Surprise! Labour’s pro-business, pro-growth affectations have turned out to be no more than skin-deep.

As is often said, allowing a more expansive North Sea won’t in itself make much difference to underlying oil and gas prices, which are mainly international in determination.

But it ought to make a sizeable difference to energy security, the balance of payments, tax revenues, investment and jobs.

If those arguments are still not enough to sway the green energy zealots of the Labour leadership, then the following certainly ought to.

According to modelling by the lobby group Offshore Energies, UK developed reserves of North Sea gas are running out at such a rate that within 10 years, Britain will be reliant on imported liquefied natural gas from the Middle East and America for more than half its total gas consumption.

We’ll still be burning some gas even as we stagger across the net zero finishing line 24 years hence – if indeed we ever do, which looks increasingly unlikely, despite the forced march imposed on Britain’s already enfeebled economy.

Sorry to keep harping on about tiny little Norway, but across the North Sea they see the UK as very much a cautionary tale. Norway, too, has some pretty challenging net zero targets, but they leave the Scope 3 emissions of their oil and gas industries outside the calculation, knowing that the country’s comparative prosperity would plummet without them.

Renewed conflict in the Middle East has again highlighted the perils of globalised supply chains and re-emphasised the importance of economic self-sufficiency.

When the facts change, said the economist John Maynard Keynes, I change my mind. What do you do, sir? Carry on regardless, comes the answer, as the ship sails blindly on towards the rocks.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: We could start fracking in the UK within months, says developer
data.description:
Gas could be fracked from Britain within months if Labour overturns a ban on the practice, a leading energy company has said.

Francis Egan, the chief executive of Australian-owned Cuadrilla Resources, said it could take as little as three months to get a gas well drilled. Production could start shortly thereafter.

The Iran war has revived the shale gas industry’s hopes of creating an “inland North Sea” in Lancashire and the East Midlands, as Britain scrambles to counter a looming energy crunch.

Cuadrilla began fracking at Preston New Road, Lancashire, in 2017. But the project was eventually scuppered when Rishi Sunak, the then prime minister, banned the practice in 2022.

“This is the second wake-up call after the Ukraine invasion, which was not that long ago. I don’t know how many more of these the politicians need,” said Mr Egan.

Cuadrilla has plugged its wells and is maintaining the site, but Mr Egan said he was ready to go back in.

“We’re keeping the lights on at Preston New Road. We’re trying to hang on to the licences,” he said.

“You could drill a well within three months in Preston New Road. It’s not a big task to get a rig, go out in the field and drill a well.”

‘The size of the prize’

Cuadrilla is not alone. Europa Oil & Gas is hoping that North Yorkshire County Council will approve next month an application to drill an appraisal well just north of Scarborough.

Planning officers have recommended that councillors approve the project, but local activists are fighting the proposal.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, praised community activists last year who had “fought back” against shale gas exploration. He promised to pass a law that would “ban fracking and send the frackers packing”.

But Mr Egan said that “nothing is forever in politics”. If the Iran war had put Britain’s North Sea oil and gas reserves back up for debate, fracking could be too.

“Shale is not like drilling a well in the North Sea – it’s like the whole North Sea. That’s the size of the prize,” he said.

Cuadrilla’s operation repeatedly breached tough limits on seismic tremors, prompting frequent mandatory shutdowns. The company also faced court challenges from anti-fracking campaigners, and on-again-off-again moratoriums from successive Conservative prime ministers.

Nigel Farage, the Reform UK leader, has supported a resumption of fracking, but there is no sign of any Labour about-turn.

Mr Egan said: “The Prime Minister seems to have outsourced everything, energy-wise, to Ed Miliband. And Ed Miliband has pretty much outsourced it to Extinction Rebellion. And it’s more extinction than rebellion, I’m afraid.”

Cuadrilla’s owner, Australian-listed mining services company AJ Lucas, has written off the value of its UK investment on its balance sheet and reduced the company to a skeleton operation.

“But they have still valued the option. And of course, given the events of the last two or three weeks, that option value has probably increased,” Mr Egan said.

Some energy analysts are sceptical that Britain’s Bowland Shale, which stretches from Lancashire across to Yorkshire and the East Midlands, is as viable for fracking as the Permian Basin in the US.

Matt Drinkwater, from analysis firm Energy Aspects, said: “Because the UK is so much more densely populated than the US, fracking is much higher cost. There’s a lot of expenditure required for mitigation of impacts on the local community, and also for the social licence to operate.”

While shale gas might be viable when gas prices are high in the coming few years, by 2028 it might be uncompetitive again and the industry would not even be up and running by then.

Mr Egan acknowledged that there was still technical work to be done to ensure that fracking the Bowland Shale would produce the right gas flows over the right time frame.

However, he said developing a domestic shale industry would give Britain lower-cost gas and an economic boost. “We’re talking tens of thousands of jobs and billions of tax revenue.”

data.title: 120-year-old British wheel factory at risk of closure
data.description:
A 120-year-old British wheel factory is at risk of closure after its German owners called in the administrators.

Moveero, which makes wheels, rims and hubs for off-road vehicles used in farming and construction, said its UK arm had become insolvent on Thursday, putting around 250 jobs at risk.

Administrators from Interpath have been appointed to run the struggling business, which trades from a historic factory in Telford, Shropshire.

Its customers include JCB and Caterpillar, among others, with its products used for the wheels of everything from earthmovers to airport tugs and tree harvesters.

The business, dating back to 1908, was part of GKN’s automotive division but was sold to German private equity firm Aurelius in 2020.

Aurelius was also behind a disastrous buyout of ethical beauty chain the Body Shop, which collapsed just five months after its new owners took control.

In a statement on Thursday, Ryan Grant, Chris Pole and Sam Birchall, administrators at Interpath, said Moveero had been battling “significant operational challenges”, including weak demand for its products and tough competition.

Rising interest rates have made buying heavy equipment more difficult for farmers and construction companies, along with trade tariffs imposed on imports by the likes of the US and China.

Moveero Ltd reported a loss of £4.4m in 2024, the latest year for which there are accounts, after sales fell 26pc to £32.6m.

The wider Moveero group, which includes businesses in the US and Denmark, continues to operate profitably and is not insolvent.

In the UK, administrators have been appointed to “protect the interests of creditors”.

A statement said this would “allow the UK businesses to continue trading while buyers for the businesses and their assets are pursued, with all staff retained during this period”.

David Geraghty, Moveero’s chief executive, said the company’s customers had also provided emergency funding that had “given us the additional time we need to continue to explore the options available”.

Mr Grant, the managing director at Interpath, added: “We have been pleased with the positive conversations and financial support provided by customers in recent days, which has ultimately provided the businesses with a crucial lifeline, enabling us to trade while we explore options for the business.”

Interpath said its “immediate priority is to seek buyers for the UK businesses and assets” and urged potential buyers to come forward as soon as possible.

Aurelius has been approached for comment.

data.title: British AI startup faces backlash over Microsoft data centre
data.description:
One of Britain’s largest artificial intelligence (AI) startups is facing a backlash over plans to build a data centre for Microsoft after local officials raised fears it would drive up energy costs for residents.

Nscale, which counts Sir Nick Clegg as a board member, has been hit with objections in Essex over concerns the power-hungry energy demands of the site will strain the local electricity grid.

The planned data centre, built on a former scaffolding warehouse in Loughton, has been billed as “the UK’s largest AI supercomputer” and will be backed by 50 megawatts of AI processors. 

One of its key customers will be Microsoft after the two companies signed a deal to develop AI power. 

However, Loughton Town council officers have now demanded fresh evidence from Nscale to prove the development’s “substantial energy demand” will not “strain local grid capacity or increase costs for residents”.

The objection was sent to officials at Epping Forest District Council who are overseeing the application. It notes that the site is 50pc higher than originally proposed and has 50pc more internal capacity.

“The proposal would require more cooling and increased energy to facilitate this application,” the objection warns.

The town planning officer has requested that Nscale submit a full new planning application to proceed with the plans. The timeline to get the project up and running has also been pushed back from this year to early 2027.

It is understood that the delay is to allow time to secure access to the most up-to-date AI processors from US chip giant Nvidia, which were only announced this month.

Nscale has been cited by the Government as a key driver of Britain’s AI infrastructure revolution.

Earlier this month, the company, led by Australian entrepreneur Josh Payne and headquartered in the UK, confirmed it had raised $2bn to expand across the US.

The business has secured investment from backers including Nvidia, Dell and Norwegian investor Aker. Its site in Loughton is expected to house thousands of Nvidia graphics processors and will count Microsoft as a key customer.

AI systems such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Anthropic’s Claude require large amounts of computing power and have fuelled hundreds of billions in investment around the world in data centre capacity.

Labour has been courting AI data centre developers as it seeks to engineer an AI infrastructure building spree in Britain and has sought to ease planning constraints.

Last week, The Telegraph disclosed that Steve Reed, the secretary for housing, had bypassed objections from councillors in Buckinghamshire to give ministers the final say over a £2bn data project.

Meanwhile, the global building blitz of AI data centres has prompted concerns over their ballooning energy demands and water use, clashing with Labour’s stated net zero agenda.

Last month, a report from the energy regulator Ofgem admitted that the total pipeline of UK data centre projects included power demands of more than 50 gigawatts (GW).

If all these projects were built and operated at full capacity, they would require more power than Britain’s peak daily electricity.

An Nscale spokesman said: “Site investigation and permit work is under way on the Loughton site, and we expect construction work to begin in the second quarter of 2026.

“While the schedule was recently updated to accommodate the installation of the latest Vera Rubin 200 technology, we expect the site to be operational in the second quarter of 2027.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour has no plan for the worst energy shock of our lives
data.description:
Keep calm and carry on – or something to that effect.

It’s week five of Donald Trump’s Iran excursion, and just when you think the president couldn’t sound any more deranged, he’s now talking about taking Kharg Island by force. 

This is an idea military strategists have compared with anything from a “suicide mission” to a “self-imposed hostage crisis”.

Either that, or Trump could decide to blow the offshore terminal to smithereens instead; such is the clear and rational thinking that pervades the White House these days.

The price of crude oil responded with another 3pc jump on Monday, taking it to $116 (£87.94) at one point – a roughly 50pc increase since hostilities began. Expect it to go much higher if the US puts boots on the ground in Iran.

Meanwhile, there is talk that repairs to damaged energy infrastructure could take between two and five years, prolonging the disruption to global supplies and keeping prices ruinously high.

Indeed, many experts already believe the Middle East conflict is shaping up to be the worst energy shock the world has ever faced.

As Fatih Birol, the head of the International Energy Agency, has warned, the Iran war has knocked out roughly 11 million barrels of oil a day compared with the loss of five million barrels during each of the two crises in 1973 and 1979.

Gas shortages have been similarly devastating, with about 140 billion cubic metres a day lost – double what was removed from the market after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

So what, might you ask, is our Government’s plan to address what could prove to be an even bigger blow to our livelihoods than both the pandemic and Vladimir Putin’s imperialist expansion on Europe’s doorstep?

The answer, it seems, is that there isn’t one. On the contrary, ministers are still – publicly, at least – persisting with the absurd stance that it is pretty much “situation normal”.

Yes, contingency plans are being drawn up. “You would expect any government to plan for eventualities where events happen in the world,” Bridget Phillipson, the Education Secretary, said breezily at the weekend.

However, “people should be reassured, ahead of the Easter holidays, just to carry on as things are”, she added, seemingly oblivious to the irony of how anxiety-inducing such words are.

While ministers attempt their desperate “nothing to see here” act for fear of “scaring people”, as Phillipson patronisingly puts it, those same people can turn on the news and see that two things are happening.

The first is the supply squeeze becoming more acute worldwide. Bangladesh may have only a few weeks of oil left, swathes of Southeast Asia have only a few months’ worth of supplies at best, parts of Africa are experiencing shortages, and so too are Australia and New Zealand.

True, many of the countries facing a critical supply squeeze are more reliant on Middle Eastern oil, while we, on the other hand, get most of our crude from the US and Norway. 

However, much more of our refined fuels are imported, which is why the price of diesel has gone up 26pc versus 15pc for unleaded petrol, so it’s only a matter of time before Europe is next.

Indeed, industry figures think the crunch is just around the corner. Wael Sawan, Shell’s chief executive, has warned of energy and fuel shortages by mid-April. That’s a fortnight away. 

Traders and fuel suppliers estimate that diesel stockpiles could halve by mid-May.

I know who I’m inclined to listen to, and it’s not the Education Secretary, nor any Cabinet member, for that matter. The Government is being far too complacent.

The second thing that people in this country can see is that while ministers dilly-dally, a growing number of countries are acting quickly and decisively, starting with sweeping tax cuts to soften the hit to household incomes.

Spain’s socialist government was among the first to move, cutting VAT on fuel, electricity and gas by more than half to 10pc as part of a €5bn (£3.8bn) emergency support package. 

Ireland cut fuel duty by a fifth, while the Polish government slashed VAT on fuels from 23pc to 8pc, and reduced excise taxes to the lowest level permitted by Brussels.

At the weekend, Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, joined in by halving fuel duty – a move expected to save motorists 26 cents a litre when they fill up their tanks – as well as unveiling a new four-stage fuel security plan to manage supply.

Other than support for those reliant on heating oil, there has been no such relief here.

Steps to bring down both energy and fuel consumption are also being rapidly introduced, though again not in the UK. 

India has shut down factories, Australia has introduced free public transport to discourage people from driving, civil servants in the Philippines are working a four-day week, and Slovenia has become the first European country to introduce rationing.

None of these represents a silver bullet, but surely any action is better than none at all? Why not start here by designating filling stations for emergency vehicles and prioritising diesel for commercial vehicles?

It is ludicrous for our Government to pretend we are somehow immune to the pressures being felt everywhere else, and it is even sillier to think we can’t handle a few small sacrifices for the greater good of the country. 

Reducing unnecessary journeys, turning off the lights, turning down the heating and going on fewer holidays would be nothing compared to the restrictions everyone endured during Covid.

Our thoroughly forgettable Prime Minister would much rather have more pointless meetings than take tough decisions – the latest with representatives from the shipping, insurance and energy industries to discuss the “importance of working closely with the private sector in tackling the crisis” – whatever that means.

For Rachel Reeves, it has reached the humiliating point where bosses are avoiding her altogether. 

First, the petrol retailers gave the Chancellor the cold shoulder because of accusations of “profiteering”, then several supermarket bosses were too busy to discuss rising food prices with her. Devoid of ideas, this Government has been reduced to populist show trials.

At the very least, it can scrap the planned fuel duty rise, yet Phillipson insists there is “no need” to. It is a farcical position to take, and it sets the scene for yet another screeching Labour policy reversal in the coming weeks. 

No wonder business bosses have stopped listening. Then again, hasn’t everyone?
