data.title: Labour risks ‘banning’ Saturday jobs, warns M&S
data.description:
The boss of Marks & Spencer has warned Labour it risked destroying Saturday jobs by overhauling workers’ rights.

Stuart Machin suggested that the introduction of new rules to guarantee weekly staff hours risked backfiring by preventing employers from hiring part-time weekend workers.

This would prove particularly problematic for those trying to take their “first step into work”, Mr Machin said.

Writing in a blog post for Alan Milburn, Sir Keir Starmer’s employment tsar, Mr Machin said: “New regulations dissuade employers from offering a shift or two a week, and the red tape around work experience often makes it prohibitive to offer.

“We’re at risk of not only disincentivising the Saturday job, but banning it.”

The warning comes as companies brace for a new wave of red tape under Labour’s sweeping workers’ rights shake-up.

Although the Employment Rights Bill received royal assent late last year, various issues are still under negotiation, and are expected to be finalised in secondary legislation.

This includes rules around guaranteed work for those on “low” and “zero-hours” contracts.

Union bosses have been urging Sir Keir to hand all workers the right to guaranteed hours.

However, retailers have raised concerns that this will make it harder to give part-time workers extra shifts.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise, the boss of Next, which employs 20,000 part-time staff, has previously said this change would leave shops “chronically overstaffed”.

He said it would force retailers to employ temporary staff to cover seasonal demand, such as the busy Christmas period, rather than offer more shifts to part-time workers who typically pick up work on Saturdays or Sundays.

This would “deprive loyal and skilled employees of income at times when they need it”, Lord Wolfson said.

Speaking on Thursday, the Next boss also raised concerns about Labour’s plans to speed up minimum wage increases for younger staff.

In its manifesto before the election, Labour pledged to pay young people the same minimum wage as older workers.

Lord Wolfson said: “It doesn’t feel like the right time. With everything that we’ve been saying about entry-level jobs being difficult, it doesn’t feel like the right time – even if it’s the right thing to do long term.”

Youth unemployment recently jumped to an 11-year high, with at least 16.1pc of 16 to 24-year-olds seeking work not yet in a job, according to the Office for National Statistics.

Increases in the minimum wage have led to almost a third of businesses reducing entry-level roles, with half looking to hire more older workers instead, according to an study from accounting firm RSM on Friday.

Mr Machin called for the Government to focus on the importance of retail to boost employment.

He said: “Retail and hospitality have been left out of most visions for economic growth. Politicians momentarily become interested in the ‘everyday economy’, but they quickly become distracted by flashier industries.

“That lack of interest is reflected in the range of cost pressures facing retail: business rates, packaging taxes, energy tariffs, national insurance rises. If we valued retail as the first rung on the jobs ladder we would surely take a different approach.”

It marks Mr Machin’s second political intervention in as many days as the retail boss also accused the Government on Thursday of driving up energy bills through a series of green levies.

Posting on LinkedIn, he said: “Over the last few years the ‘policy costs’ on our energy bill have skyrocketed.

“These are the tariffs that [the Government] place on our bills to fund their policies, and have nothing to do with the price of oil or gas. They now make up over half our bill. It’s just not sustainable for UK businesses.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British factories brace for an energy crisis worse than the 1970s
data.description:
Europe’s fuel supplies could be squeezed within days, and governments need to act.

That is the ominous warning from Wael Sawan, the boss of Shell boss, who this week said “a ripple effect” could see the continent’s energy security hit “as we get into April”.

A growing number of governments across the world are already introducing exceptional measures to limit the amount of fuel people use as the world reels from the Middle East energy supply shock triggered by war in Iran.

South Korea’s residents have been told to drive less, take shorter showers and use washing machines and vacuum cleaners only on the weekend.

In Sri Lanka, the government has declared that public institutions should take Wednesday off. The island nation has also implemented fuel rationing, with drivers now limited to buying 15 litres of petrol or diesel a day for private cars.

Thailand, with its tropical climate, has called on workers to swap suits for t-shirts in an effort to reduce the use of air conditioning. Swimming pools are no longer being heated in Mauritius and fountains have been turned off at the government’s direction.

All that may seem like a distant concern – something affecting far-off lands with vastly different economies and climates to ours.

Yet restrictions are already beginning to hit Europe. Slovenia became the first EU country this week to introduce fuel rationing, limiting drivers to 50 litres a day.

Here in the UK, ministers insist fuel supplies are secure and are urging people to go about their days as normal. Yet if this crisis drags on, that may not last.

The Telegraph has reported that strict speed limits could be imposed under the National Emergency Plan for Fuel, should ministers choose to activate it.

Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said home-working guidance may also be likely if the Government decides to bring in measures to curb energy consumption in the coming months.

“If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that,” he says.

“If you do not have enough fuel, governments have to think very carefully about how they use the fuel they have, what is still coming in, and what they hold in inventories,” says Callum MacPherson, the head of commodities at Investec.

Ashley Kelty, of investment bank Panmure Liberum, said limits on the amount of gas factories can use may also be considered.

Even if the Government doesn’t step in, manufacturers may adopt self-imposed limits as costs skyrocket.

The average annual electricity bill for small industrial and commercial businesses has risen by 20pc, or £96,000, to £578,000 since the war in Iran began, figures from Cornwall Insight show.

For those businesses that use gas – including chemical producers, glass and ceramics factories and metalworks – the pain is even worse. Average annual gas bills have risen by nearly 60pc to just more than £1m.

“Many of these companies are already battling slimmer margins, so this rise in energy costs is not something they can simply absorb,” Jacob Briggs, from Cornwall Insight, says.

“For some firms, this could mean the difference between investing in growth this year or shelving their plans entirely, and for others, high bills could force some very difficult economic decisions.”

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, told The Telegraph last week there was a “high risk” that factories would start voluntarily cutting production in the coming weeks because of high energy costs.

Kelty fears that the impact of the crisis on the UK economy could be “horrendous”, with the automotive and steel manufacturing sectors the worst affected.

He said: “Even if the rockets stop tomorrow, the problem is that it’s going to take a month to get back to any form of normality in terms of restarting oil production and getting all the tankers out of the Gulf and to their original destinations.”

The UK will face a “very challenging” winter, as even if the conflict comes to an end, it will struggle to refill its gas storage, Kelty added.

All this is set to weigh on the economy. If the energy crisis continues and Brent crude oil remains at around $100 a barrel, Capital Economics has forecast that the UK will enter a mild recession by the end of the year.

Jennifer McKeown, chief global economist from Capital Economics, said: “The energy-intensive sectors of industry, hotels and retail are likely to be hit the hardest.

“The sharp increase in the cost of energy is going to mean that it is quite likely that industrial production will need to be cut back.”

In the summer of 2022, as energy prices soared following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, many European nations imposed policies to curb oil and gas consumption.

Measures at the time included restrictions on air conditioning and reduced lighting for national monuments and advertising boards.

By late 2022, Britons were encouraged to cut energy use during peak times, with some electricity providers offering reduced costs in an effort to encourage households to shift consumption to off-peak hours.

Restrictions were far more draconian during the Arab oil crisis of the 1970s. In 1973, drivers were using fuel ration books and were advised to reduce their maximum speed to 50mph.

By early 1974, fuel shortages and rationing meant that many industrial businesses were operating with a three-day working week and TV channels were shut off after 10.30pm.

Fatih Birol, executive director of the International Energy Agency (IEA), has said that the current energy supply shock is worse than the 1970s. “No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction,” he has warned. “So there is a need for global efforts.”

The IEA has called for car-free Sundays globally and urged people to work from home three days a week in order to conserve fuel.

Ministers in Britain are understood to have found this intervention unhelpful. With the Government convinced the UK has enough fuel for the moment, their biggest concern is to avoid panic buying or unnecessary changes in business patterns that could create fresh problems.

However, disruption to energy supplies looks likely to continue. QatarEnergy, which operates the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility, said on Tuesday that it was preparing to declare “force ‌majeure” on some fuel contracts.

The crisis measure would allow it to stop shipments agreed under contract without facing any legal liability. Italy, Belgium, South Korea ⁠and China will be affected.

MacPherson said: “If this went on and on, and we had to live in a world where there is much less oil than we have been used to in the past, people would have to travel less, and when they do travel, it would be more expensive.”

While Britain may have escaped the worst of it so far, the threat of an energy-triggered economic crunch looms.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”

data.title: Majority of Labour voters back drilling in the North Sea
data.description:
A majority of Labour voters support drilling for oil in the North Sea, a new poll has found.

In a blow to Ed Miliband, a survey has revealed that supporters of drilling outnumber the opponents by more than two to one amongst his party’s voters.

The Energy Secretary has repeatedly dismissed suggestions that Britain should ramp up domestic oil and gas production, even as the crisis in the Middle East has piled pressure on global supplies.

But a poll by campaign group Looking for Growth suggests Labour’s supporters – and a majority of voters in the country – disagree with him.

The poll found that support for drilling for oil in the North Sea stood at about 57pc amongst all voters, compared to only 15pc who were against.

Within this, support amongst Labour voters stood at 51pc while 21pc were opposed. Another 22pc said they neither agreed nor disagreed, with 5pc saying they didn’t know.

Even amongst backers of the eco-focused Green Party, supporters for North Sea drilling narrowly outnumbered opponents by 38pc to 33pc.

Support amongst Conservative and Reform voters topped 70pc.

Some 49pc of Labour voters also indicated they supported onshore fracking of oil and gas – although just 40pc were happy for it to take place in their area.

Lawrence Newport, the chief executive of Looking for Growth, said: “There has been no government plan to reduce bills, have abundant energy and stable long-term power.

“Instead, politicians have adopted policies that have left the lives of millions across the country at the mercy of international events.

“When successive governments give in to the consultants, lawyers and lobbyists, the energy bills of the British people pay the price.

“This new polling shows that voters of all parties want a broken system fixed and to be prioritised again.”

Looking for Growth’s poll also asked voters about nuclear power and found that 61pc supported it, compared to 14pc who opposed it.

The debate over North Sea drilling has intensified in recent weeks following the US-Iran conflict, which sent energy prices soaring.

The RAC said on Tuesday the crisis had pushed the cost of filling up a typical family car with diesel beyond £100 for the first time since 2022.

The cost of filling up a petrol car has similarly increased by around £11, bringing the total to £85.

Most supporters of increasing domestic oil and gas production say it is unlikely to directly lower costs, as both commodities are priced on international markets, but that it could improve energy security, generate higher tax revenues and protect jobs.

Mr Miliband has introduced a ban on new drilling in the North Sea and has so far resisted pressure to rethink his position.

Speaking to Labour MPs this month, he reportedly said: “Gas is bought and sold on the international market, so whether it comes from the North Sea or is imported, it is charged at the same price.”

He went on to praise the amount of money raised by a renewable energy auction earlier this year, saying technology such as wind turbines and solar panels was “homegrown power that we can rely on”.

The Conservatives accused the Energy Secretary of dogma and called on the Government to cut taxes on domestic energy production.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said earlier this week: “It is unforgivable that Labour are continuing with Ed Miliband’s mad plan to shut down our own energy supplies in the North Sea in the middle of an energy crisis.

“Keir Starmer is too weak to stand up to Ed Miliband, who has allowed radical activists to make us weaker, poorer and more dependent on foreign imports with higher emissions through endless lawfare.”

A Government spokesman said: “Regardless of where it comes from, oil and gas is sold on international markets, which set the price for British billpayers – making us a price taker.

“The only way to truly protect ourselves from these price spikes is to get off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel markets.”

data.title: £160m wiped off Currys as boss behind turnaround quits
data.description:
More than £160m was wiped off the value of Currys after the electrical goods giant announced the departure of its chief executive.

Alex Baldock will leave after eight years running the retailer, having spearheaded a successful turnaround and fended off two takeover attempts from foreign suitors.

Confirmation of his exit led to shares in Currys falling by more than 11pc on Thursday, the biggest drop in two years.

Before Thursday’s announcement, shares had risen by more than 160pc in the past three years, with the business now worth £1.33bn.

He has been credited with overseeing a revival in the company’s fortunes after it struggled for years in the wake of a 2014 merger with Carphone Warehouse.

Analysts at City broker Panmure Liberum said Mr Baldock had left Currys a “significantly stronger business than the one he inherited in 2018, when his turnaround strategy was first introduced”.

As well as navigating competition from Amazon and China’s e-commerce giants, he also successfully repelled takeover interest from Chinese online shopping empire JD.com and Wall Street activist investor Elliott in 2024.

Panmure Liberum said Currys had “moved from a net-debt to a net-cash position and restored margins back to pre-Covid levels in the face of tough macroeconomic conditions”.

Ian Dyson, the Currys chairman, said Mr Baldock had “achieved a huge amount” during his tenure, including “transforming the business in the face of some difficult headwinds”.

He added: “Currys is very well positioned for future success with a strategy that is clearly working, great financial health and a very strong leadership team.”

Adam Tomlinson, of German private bank Berenberg, said Mr Baldock had “led the most impressive UK retail turnaround of recent years”.

Mr Baldock is leaving to take an external position but will stay in post while the search for a successor is carried out.

He said: “My eight years at Currys have been simply the best of my career, and I’ll always be a loyal Currys customer, advocate and shareholder.

“Currys’ next chapter can be its most exciting yet. But it’s time for someone else to steer the business there, and time for me to move on to a new opportunity.”

As well as announcing Mr Baldock’s departure, Currys insisted it had traded in line with expectations since January, despite the global turmoil caused by the conflict in the Middle East.

It expects yearly profits to settle between £180m and £190m, an increase of up to 17pc on the previous year.

data.title: Don’t cut the ‘crown jewels’: Six ways the BBC could actually save money
data.description:
Reports that the BBC is to cut all but one of its permanent staff covering the most significant moments in our national life – such as royal weddings and funerals, state openings of Parliament and military anniversaries – have prompted howls of dismay.

The highly regarded Claire Popplewell, whom I interviewed last year, is said to be the sole survivor at BBC Studios Events, which has been described as the “crown jewel of live British broadcasting”. Future pomp and pageantry looks like it will largely be covered by a team of freelancers on an ad-hoc basis, rather than a team of seasoned pros who just last week won a Royal Television Society award for their coverage of the 80th anniversary of the liberation of Auschwitz.

Instead of gutting one of its most esteemed departments, here are some other ways that the cash-strapped corporation – which is in the process of renegotiating its charter with ministers – could make much-needed cost savings. Matt Brittin, the incoming director-general, may wish to take note.

Cull BBC Verify

BBC Verify, the corporation’s fact-checking unit, was the brainchild of Deborah Turness, its former news chief, and was set up in 2023. In the years since, the team of 60 journalists has become one of the BBC’s most controversial departments. It has variously been described by critics as having made factual errors and promoted flawed journalism, as well as a Left-wing bias and hostility to Israel in its reporting on the wars in Gaza and the wider Middle East.

Cut back on Glastonbury…

The BBC sent 550 people to cover Glastonbury last summer, and dispatches a similar number of people to Worthy Farm each year. Despite this huge presence at June’s festival, including that of Tim Davie, the director-general at the time, the live stream for a controversial performance by rap act Bob Vylan was not cut when frontman Bobby Vylan led the crowd in chants of “Death, death to the IDF [Israel Defense Forces]”. Perhaps if there were fewer people on a glorified jolly, such errors might be avoided in future.

…and ditch the Baftas

From one catastrophic failure of BBC staff broadcasting offensive language at a live event to another. Despite last month’s Baftas being broadcast on a two-hour delay, the BBC aired an audible shout of the N-word in its coverage. The slur was involuntarily said by John Davidson, the Tourette syndrome campaigner and inspiration for the film I Swear, when black actors Michael B Jordan and Delroy Lindo were presenting the night’s first award. Davidson said he was mortified and, as director Kirk Jones told me afterwards, was “let down on many levels” – not least by the BBC. What’s more, there is little evidence that the public even wants to watch what is essentially an industry awards ceremony. Just three million viewers watched this year, as opposed to the eight million who regularly tune into Call the Midwife in the same Sunday timeslot.

Lose the middle managers

According to the BBC’s 2023-24 annual report, the corporation has 236 “senior leaders” whose salaries are in excess of £100,000. Would things at Broadcasting House actually be any worse if, instead of cutting the BBC Studios Events team, the same amount was saved from the ranks of middle-management? Presenters have scorned recent initiatives to teach them about developing “tools for psychological safety” rather than, erm, letting them get on and do their jobs.

Put EastEnders out of its misery

EastEnders celebrated its 40th anniversary in February last year, and the long-running soap seems to be going through something of a midlife crisis. Like its ITV rivals Coronation Street and Emmerdale, changes in audience habits mean that viewing figures are in precipitous decline, and have been for some time. But just four years ago, when the pattern had set in, the BBC started filming EastEnders on a brand-new £87m set. That now looks like folly. How many years’ worth of Trooping the Colour might that have funded?

Don’t lose the Donald Trump lawsuit

Everything looks like chump change when compared with Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.6bn) lawsuit, which he launched after The Telegraph revealed that Panorama had selectively edited his speech on the day of the Capitol riots in January 2021. For context, the BBC gets less than £4bn each year from the licence fee. The corporation has urged an American court to dismiss the US president’s case, on the grounds that the programme was never aired in the US and that, clearly, it did nothing to harm his re-election in November 2024. The case is set to go to trial in February.

data.title: Marmite sold to the Americans in £50bn deal
data.description:
Marmite will fall into American hands after its owner agreed to a $66bn (£50bn) deal with a US food giant.

The brand, known for its love-it-or-hate-it slogan, is one of several supermarket staples being sold by Unilever to Baltimore-based spice and sauce maker McCormick.

Other brands changing hands under the deal include Hellmann’s mayonnaise, Knorr stock cubes, Bovril, Colman’s mustard and Pot Noodle. They will join McCormick’s stable, which includes French’s mustard and Schwartz herbs.

Fernando Fernández, the boss of Unilever, said the deal would create a “global flavour powerhouse”.

The combined company will be 65pc-owned by Unilever but will operate under McCormick’s name and leadership, with its headquarters in the US. The new company will also be listed in New York.

Unilever and McCormick said they hoped to find $600m of cost savings from the deal, a forecast sure to raise concerns about potential job losses.

Marmite was founded in Burton-on-Trent in 1902, utilising leftover yeast from a local brewery, and is still made in the Staffordshire town today. Colman’s started in Norwich in 1814 and continues to be made from mustard seeds grown in the area.

Brendan Foley, McCormick’s chairman, said a “big portion” of savings would come from the power to negotiate better prices with suppliers, given the bigger orders that will be placed.

However, he refused to rule out factory closures in the UK. Asked about the potential to move manufacturing out of Britain, he said the company may see “synergies play out” in manufacturing, distribution and media.

“When we think about getting more detail about those areas, it is probably detail yet to be shared,” he added.

News of the potential sale of iconic British brands Marmite and Colman’s sparked a backlash earlier this month. John Farrand, the managing director of the Guild of Fine Food, said the companies would “become a number on a spreadsheet.”

It follows other US takeovers of historic British brands such as Cadbury and Lea & Perrins.

The sale leaves Unilever focused on home and beauty brands such as Dove, Vaseline and Lynx.

Unilever recently demerged its ice cream business, spinning it out as The Magnum Ice Cream Company. The new entity, which was the result of an almost two-year-long process, is now home to Ben & Jerry’s and Cornetto, as well as the eponymous ice cream brand.

As well as a majority shareholding, Unilever will get $15.7bn in cash under the terms of the deal.

Mr Fernández said the McCormick deal was “another decisive step in sharpening our portfolio and accelerating our strategy towards high-growth categories”.

He said: “We are unlocking trapped value through a growth-led separation of foods, creating a scaled, global flavour powerhouse.

“By combining Unilever Foods’ iconic leading brands and global reach with McCormick’s exceptional portfolio, category expertise and capabilities, we are establishing a focused, high-quality business with significant top-line growth and value creation potential.”

However, shares in Unilever fell more than 7pc on news of the transaction.

Chris Beckett, an analyst at wealth manager Quilter Cheviot, said: “The market, so far, has not reacted well to the news.

“Completion is not expected until mid-2027, which is a long time and introduces risks that mean it could be stopped.”

Unilever has been on a major drive to trim down its sprawling portfolio of food brands.

It sold its spreads business in 2017, which included I Can’t Believe It’s Not Butter and Flora, and in 2022, it offloaded most of its tea business, including Lipton and PG Tips.

Brendan Foley, McCormick chairman, said: “The Unilever Foods business is one we have long admired, with a portfolio that complements our existing business, capabilities and long-term vision.

“This combination will create a diversified flavour leader with a robust growth profile that remains differentiated by its focus on flavouring calories while others compete for them.”

As well as French’s, a sweet mustard typically associated with hot dogs, McCormick also makes Frank’s Red Hot and Cholula hot sauces and various spice blends, including Old Bay seasoning.

data.title: BBC knew about Scott Mills allegations almost a year ago
data.description:
The BBC has admitted it knew of allegations against Scott Mills almost a year ago but failed to investigate, The Telegraph can reveal.

The Radio 2 presenter’s BBC contract was terminated on Monday following allegations about his “personal conduct”.

It was reported that the sacking related to allegations of serious sexual offences committed by Mills against a teenage boy who was under 16 between 1997 and 2000.

But The Telegraph can reveal that a former BBC presenter contacted the corporation in May 2025 to say she had received information about alleged “inappropriate communications” involving Mills and asked whether the broadcaster was “ever aware of or involved in any related matters”.

Anna Brees, a freelance journalist, also asked the BBC whether it had ever received any “formal or informal complaints” about Mills “relating to safeguarding, inappropriate conduct or harassment” and whether it had ever conducted an internal investigation into him.

She did not receive a response. On Tuesday, the BBC admitted her information “should have been followed up and we should have asked further questions”.

In a statement to The Telegraph, the broadcaster added: “We apologise for this and will look into why this did not happen.”

There is no suggestion that the inquiries by Ms Brees related to the same alleged victim whose complaint to the police led to Mills being questioned under caution in 2018.

But the BBC faces questions about why it did nothing in response to a claim of wrongful behaviour by a star presenter who later had to be dismissed.

‘Inappropriate messages’

The emergence of the email sent by Ms Brees to the BBC press office shows that the corporation was made aware of unspecified allegations about Mills’s conduct more than 10 months ago.

Ms Brees, who presented news programmes for both the BBC and ITV from 2003-2011, said: “Someone who knew I used to work for the BBC volunteered some information about Scott Mills messaging someone inappropriately.

“It was a credible source and I was concerned so I decided to contact the BBC to make them aware of it and see if they came back with anything. I didn’t get a response.”

The BBC said in a statement: “We received a press query in 2025 which included limited information. This should have been followed up and we should have asked further questions. We apologise for this and will look into why this did not happen.

“More broadly, we would always urge anyone who has concerns or information to raise it with us.”

The BBC has been accused of responding too slowly – or not at all – to claims of inappropriate behaviour by stars including Jimmy Savile, Huw Edwards and Gregg Wallace.

It was reported on Tuesday night that the BBC knew about the allegation which led to Mills’s sacking, first reported by The Mirror.

The Sun on Sunday revealed that an investigation was being carried out into an unnamed radio presenter in March of that year, but BBC bosses chose not to take Mills off air.

A source told The Sun that Mills told bosses he had been questioned by police but they allowed him to carry on working while the investigation continued.

The BBC launched its investigation after the complaint was raised with the broadcaster again, The Sun reported.

Allegation made in 2016

The allegation which led to Mills’s sacking was made to Hampshire and Isle of Wight Constabulary in December 2016 and then passed to the Metropolitan Police.

Mills was interviewed under caution in July 2018 and a full file of evidence was passed to the Crown Prosecution Service (CPS) but the case was closed in May 2019 because of insufficient evidence.

The Telegraph understands that the alleged victim was unhappy with the CPS decision not to bring charges.

He requested a review of the decision, but it also concluded there was insufficient evidence.

On Tuesday the charity Neuroblastoma UK announced it had dropped Mills as a patron.

Mills – who had worked for the BBC since 1998 – will no longer host the Radio 2 breakfast show, which he took over from Zoe Ball last year.

The 53-year-old was last heard on air on March 25 and had been scheduled to return the next day, telling listeners that he would be “back tomorrow”.

In an internal email seen by The Sun, Rhodri Talfan Davies, the acting BBC director-general, told staff: “I hope you all understand that we are not able to share any more information.”

data.title: Thames Water to escape new penalties until 2030 under rescue deal
data.description:
Thames Water is set to escape new fines until 2030 under a deal agreed between creditors and the industry watchdog to save the stricken company.

Ofwat, which regulates the sector, is planning to shield the struggling utility from financial penalties linked to poor performance in return for a commitment from creditors to invest cash in the business for the next four years.

The controversial plan would carve out Thames from a penalty scheme run by Ofwat which levies fines if water companies fall behind on key metrics such as leaks and sewer flooding.

Thames is struggling under a £20bn debt pile and will run out of money again in October without the new creditor agreement.

However, the penalties deal has angered campaigners, who say the watchdog is failing in its duty to hold water companies to account.

Feargal Sharkey, the clean water campaigner, accused Ofwat of shirking its responsibility to regulate the industry.

“Ofwat has proved yet again it is incapable, unwilling to and simply does not want to properly regulate the greed, the abuse of customers and the financial engineering that is destroying this industry and the environment,” he said.

The plan, which has been put forward by creditors including US hedge funds Elliott Management and Silver Point, first went to Ofwat for approval in June 2025.

The creditors, known as London & Valley Water, provided the utility with £3bn in emergency funding last year.

As part of the deal, some creditors like Elliott would accept a 30pc write-down in their debt while more junior creditors would lose all their money.

The creditors defended the agreement and said it would mean “clear accountability” if things went wrong.

A spokesman said: “All outstanding fines will be paid. Regulators will have enhanced transparency and Thames Water will have clear accountability for reducing pollution and improving environmental outcomes against stretching performance targets.”

Utilities such as Thames Water have to pay penalties when they fail to meet performance targets set by Ofwat for the year.

Under the new proposals, first reported by The Financial Times, these penalties would be replaced with a formal commitment to invest in Thames Water.

This would allow Ofwat to continue with investigations and penalties but any cash raised from them would be spent on fixing issues with the business rather than returned to the Treasury.

The utility could still face separate fines from other regulators like the Environment Agency and would still have to reckon with possible legal cases. All outstanding fines would also have to be paid.

The Government is reportedly keen to keep Thames Water out of temporary nationalisation but the utility has only been kept afloat so far by the £3bn emergency loan, which is expected to cost the utility an extra £800m in interest payments.

Thames Water said that if Ofwat agreed a deal, creditors had offered the company an additional £6.6bn of debt at a lower rate of interest than the emergency loan, as well as £3.35bn of equity.

A spokesman for Thames Water said: “Thames Water remains focused on securing a market-led solution which delivers improvements for customers and the environment as soon as practicable, whilst making progress with our operational and financial turnaround plan.”

An Ofwat spokesperson said: “We continue to engage with London & Valley Water, and are reviewing their plans carefully to assess whether they deliver a turnaround in the company’s operational performance and strengthen its financial resilience to the benefit of customers and the environment.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Honda and Sony scrap £70,000 electric car in last-minute about-turn
data.description:
Honda and Sony have abruptly cancelled plans for a £70,000 electric vehicle (EV) amid a slowdown in demand.

The two Japanese giants said on Wednesday they would scrap the launch of Afeela, an electric sedan that had been scheduled to go on sale later this year.

The duo had established a joint venture, known as Sony Honda Mobility, four years ago in an effort to tap into rising demand for battery-powered cars. The Afeela was scheduled to be built at a plant in Ohio, with production trials starting last year.

However, the cancellation is a surprise because the carmakers had been so close to launching the vehicle.

Afeela showrooms had been open for customer demonstrations and sales were expected to begin in the US in the coming months.

At the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas, just weeks ago, they also revealed plans for a new SUV and claimed its new vehicles would “evolve” modern cars from “driver-centric machines into intelligent partners”.

‘Challenging earnings situation’

Honda disclosed a $15.7bn (£11.7bn) write-down on its EV division this month, blaming a slowdown in demand for battery-powered vehicles in the US.

Honda said it had invested heavily in electric cars as “striving for carbon neutrality is a responsibility Honda, as a manufacturer of mobility products, must fulfil for the future”.

But it admitted its cars business division had “fallen into an extremely challenging earnings situation”.

The Japanese business said Donald Trump’s tariffs had hit the profitability of its vehicle sales in America, while a decision by the president to scrap EV incentives had dampened consumer demand.

It also warned of rising competition from cheap Chinese rivals that offer greater value to consumers.

It announced it had cancelled three of its own EVs that had been planned for the US market, and this week confirmed the end of its Afeela project with Sony.

Sony Honda Mobility said on Wednesday that Honda’s “reassessment of its automobile electrification strategy” had “fundamentally altered” plans to develop and launch the Afeela.

It said the venture’s owners had agreed to launch a review of the plans for Sony Honda Mobility.

Honda, Japan’s second-biggest carmaker, has repeatedly lowered its ambitions for EVs as it battles waning demand and competition from China, switching instead to launching new hybrid models to appeal to more drivers.

It also cut its EV sales target last May from 30pc of vehicles to 20pc. Toshihiro Mibe, Honda’s chief executive, said at the time it would also reduce investment in electrification by about three trillion Japanese yen (£14bn) by 2031.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The green-belt village about to fall under the shadow of Europe’s largest data centre
data.description:
“The peace, quiet and openness of the country.” That’s why Danny Leach and his family first moved to North Ockendon, on the north-east side of Greater London, back in 2005.

Despite being barely outside the M25 and a part of the London Borough of Havering, the village and surrounding countryside are remarkably quiet.

The fenland surrounding the village is undeveloped and, considering its proximity to the capital, surprisingly biodiverse.

“I’ve got nearly 100 species of birds that visit my garden, including kingfishers, barn owls and tawny owls,” says Leach. “There are also great crested newts and foxes. On a summer evening, I can hear the frogs croaking.”

Part of the reason such an environment can exist so close to a city of 10 million is the Metropolitan Green Belt, which, since its creation in 1938, has protected the countryside near London and prevented urban sprawl.

Now, a proposed Green Belt-busting development could change the area’s character forever.

Havering council recently launched a consultation on “East Havering Data Centre”, a hyperscale AI facility that would make North Ockendon home to the largest data centre in Europe.

The facility would use 540 acres of existing farmland and require 600 megawatts of electricity to operate – the same amount of energy needed to run one million homes.

Developer Digital Reef says the site will include around 84 acres of data centre buildings, along with a 280-acre ecological park.

Residents were outraged to learn that the consultation period for the 2,700-page proposal would last only 30 days, clashing with the May elections, and that Havering council were processing the project via a “Local Development Order”, a special type of planning tool that allows conventional planning permission steps to be skipped.

Labour and Conservative members of the council’s strategic planning committee advocated a longer, 12-week consultation period, but were outvoted by the Havering Residents Association, a local party that runs the council.

Data centres are essentially large warehouses filled with specialist computing equipment that processes AI requests submitted by users on their phones or computers. East Havering Data Centre is one of hundreds of these projects proposed near Britain’s major cities to power the growing demand for AI, many of which will be built on green-belt land.

Data centres require large amounts of energy and water to operate, raising concerns that construction plans could put pressure on electricity and water infrastructure and drive up household bills.

Leach is one of many North Ockendon residents who are unhappy with the proposal. His property, which backs on to the site, will be particularly affected.

‘People move to the country for a reason’

“It’s a big shock,” he says. “People move to the countryside for a reason, and then suddenly you have something the size of a battleship going up at the end of your garden.”

Local businesses are also worried. Daniel Anthony has run Top Meadow Golf Club his entire working life, taking over from his father.

Since launching a nine-hole golf club in 1985, the family has grown the business into an 18-hole club and 20-room hotel that employs 50 people.

“Everything, everything, everything has been reinvested from day one,” says Anthony. He is worried the project is putting a lifetime of work at risk.

Today, Anthony says the club makes most of its money from functions hosted at the hotel and restaurant: “We do anything from birthdays and anniversaries to funerals, which are a big one for us because it’s only two and a half miles to the nearest crematorium.”

East Havering Data Centre will be built directly across the road from Top Meadow. Anthony is worried that the facility will be a blight on the club’s prized views of the countryside. “It’s the view really; that’s what people come for,” he says.

He is also concerned about industrial traffic and possible traffic diversions during the construction. A traffic diversion could extend the journey from Top Meadow to the nearby crematorium from two and a half to 10 miles, threatening the club’s funeral and wake business.

The rise of data centres

Developer Digital Reef estimates the facility will take four years to construct – longer than most data centre projects.

Ed Griffiths, of construction consultancy Barbour ABI, says construction is relatively fast for most data centres, with build programmes averaging 19 to 30 months, depending on scale and power requirements.

Sites near large cities are ideal for AI data centres, as user requests can be processed more quickly.

“Urban fringe locations are common for data centres, particularly where they provide proximity to users while still offering enough space and infrastructure to support large facilities,” says Griffiths.

This makes the green-belt land that surrounds many of Britain’s cities tempting for developers.

Data provided to The Telegraph by Barbour ABI indicate that 13 of the 100 largest data centres planned in the UK would fall within the green belt.

These green-belt data centres have a combined value of just over £10bn, representing an outsized share of the 100 largest planned projects at 19pc of the £52bn total.

Data centre construction is a priority for the Government, which views the facilities as essential for turning Britain into an “AI superpower” and for growing the economy.

In a January 2025 speech, Sir Keir Starmer said AI will be “mainlined into the veins of the nation” and has classed data centres as “critical national infrastructure”.

‘Destroying a rich ecosystem’

Many developers include environmental concessions, such as the East Havering Data Centre’s “ecology park” in London, in their planning applications as a sweetener for nearby communities.

However, Ian Pirie, a Havering Friends of the Earth co-ordinator, is sceptical of their benefits.

“Developers think that they can just tear up all that land and then dig somewhere else, plant some trees, and they’ve restored the ecosystem. It’s complete nonsense,” says Pirie.

“They are destroying an already existing, rich ecosystem. It would take another 100 years to have what we’ve got there now.”

Another local concern is the data centre’s potential water usage. The facilities often use water cooling to prevent computers from overheating.

Digital Reef has proposed that excess heat generated by the data centre will be redirected to heat local homes and a novel 12-acre indoor avocado farm.

But residents are unconvinced, telling The Telegraph that the local water supply is already temperamental and that industrial usage could push it to breaking point.

A spokesman for North Ockendon Residents’ Association says: “The water supply here is very old, under-maintained pipe work. If they were to then start taking thousands of gallons a day and something happened, we would be without water.”

Griffiths, who works at Barbour ABI, says access to grid power is the most important factor in selecting a site for a data centre, followed by water availability.

Data centres’ use of energy and water resources could have a national impact. Ireland, which is a hub for trans-Atlantic data cables and hosts the European headquarters of American tech giants such as Amazon and Google, has prioritised its data centre infrastructure.

In 2024, Ireland’s data centres used 22pc of its total electricity supply, more than the 18pc used by the country’s urban homes, according to the Central Statistics Office. There are concerns that a similar build-out in the UK would drive up energy costs, squeezing consumers.

A spokesman for Havering Local Planning Authority says that a full evidence base for East Havering Data Centre would be considered as part of the application, including assessments on biodiversity, ecology, economic impact, transport, water usage and use of the green belt. 

Digital Reef could not be reached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe backs Tory plans to scrap net zero taxes
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe has backed Conservative plans to scrap net zero taxes in a move that would slash energy bills for households and businesses.

The billionaire founder of petrochemicals giant Ineos said he welcomed plans by the Conservative Party to completely scrap levies on carbon emissions if it wins the next election.

Sir Jim said he was in favour of policies that would “keep the lights on” and make energy affordable and green “in a way that is practical and sustainable”.

He previously warned that carbon taxes were hurting European industry because they made domestic products far more expensive than those coming from countries such as China.

Unveiling the plans to axe levies on carbon emissions, Kemi Badenoch, the Conservative leader, said they were “killing British industry and fatally weakening our national resilience”.

Under the Conservatives’ proposals, industrial firms would no longer have to buy so-called carbon credits to compensate for emissions generated under the Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS).

The changes would also make electricity cheaper for all consumers by cutting the cost of gas-fired power generation by around one third.

The proposals are supported by major industrial companies, including oil giant ExxonMobil and chemicals producer Huntsman Corporation, as well as industry bodies such as the Chemical Industries Association and Ceramics UK.

However, the plans are likely to provoke outrage among climate campaigners who claim that carbon taxes are essential to deterring investment in fossil fuel projects that cause global warming.

It would also be likely to put the UK at odds with the European Union, which is introducing a “carbon border” mechanism.

Mrs Badenoch said: “I have heard from countless bosses how carbon taxes and green levies have made doing business in Britain much, much harder than it needs to be.

“It’s time to reverse decades of deindustrialisation by doing what Keir Starmer lacks the backbone to do: axe the carbon tax in its entirety.

“We all want to leave a better environment for the next generation, but it is madness to pursue that goal by killing British industry and fatally weakening our national resilience.”

Paul Greenwood, chairman of ExxonMobil’s UK business, which runs the Fawley refinery in Hampshire, said his company “pays hundreds of millions of pounds in CO2 costs every year that many of our international competitors do not pay”.

He said: “We support policies that create a level playing field and allow us to continue building the strong refineries on which this country depends.

“Continuing as-is risks both jobs and our national security.”

Peter Huntsman, president of Huntsman Corporation, which runs the at-risk Wilton aniline plant in Teesside, said: “The carbon tax, along with banning domestic energy production, is driving the deindustrialisation of Britain, pushing up costs, weakening competitiveness and destroying jobs and livelihoods.”

Green backlash

However, Doug Parr, chief scientist at Greenpeace UK, warned against the proposals, saying: “You can construct carbon taxes in different ways that meet your policy goals, including by returning money to taxpayers through dividends.

“The question for the Conservatives now is how are they going to fill the hole in tax revenues that this would create, and, more importantly, if they say they believe in climate change and scientists are correct that it is one of the biggest threats to our future, then what are they actually going to do about it?

“At the moment, they are stripping away all the policy measures that would tackle the problem.”

Mandatory carbon taxes were first introduced under the coalition government led by David Cameron, the Conservative prime minister, in 2013 via the Carbon Price Support (CPS).

It was designed to encourage the phase-out of coal-fired power generation, but also applies to gas-fired plants. Following the closure of Britain’s last coal power plant in September 2024, some experts have now called for the CPS’s abolition.

According to the climate think tank Ember, the average cost of gas-fired power generation was £80.62 per megawatt-hour in February. Of this, it said £25.53 was carbon taxes.

This is significant because gas plants set electricity prices for all generators more than 90pc of the time, meaning it inflates the cost British consumers pay for a range of types of power.

The emissions trading scheme, meanwhile, has been in place since 2021 and allocates carbon credits to industrial businesses that generate emissions.

If they generate emissions beyond the amount of credits they have, they must buy more from others on the market.

The carbon allowances are then tightened each year to encourage more firms to adopt carbon-free manufacturing processes.

Trade bodies such as British Glass and Ceramics UK have warned that many green technologies needed to decarbonise their industries are not yet mature or cost-effective.

It means, they say, that companies are effectively doomed to pay crippling taxes that have made them uncompetitive compared to foreign rivals.

The Chemical Industries Association has also blamed carbon taxes for a spate of plant closures in recent years.

Chris McDonald MP, Labour’s Industry Minister, said: “Kemi Badenoch has exposed her own hypocrisy given she personally introduced these measures as a Tory Treasury minister.

“It’s a total embarrassment for her to, yet again, be railing against her own work in government. Her new pledge is wrong, and it would hammer industry.

“This multibillion-pound unfunded spending commitment has echoes of Liz Truss and would leave working people picking up the bill. The failed Conservative Party haven’t changed, and their sums still don’t add up.

“Through our Industrial Strategy, Labour is slashing electricity costs for business by 25pc. Our long-term plan, in partnership with industry, will boost investment, create good skilled jobs, and make Britain the best place to do business.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe’s chemical plant suffers £515m loss
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s chemicals empire racked up €593m (£515m) of losses last year, as it warned of further pain from the war in Iran.

Ineos blamed soaring energy costs and Donald Trump’s tariffs as losses jumped from €71.1m the year prior, highlighting mounting economic strain on the billionaire’s sprawling business operations.

As well as a lack of profit, Ineos’s debt pile also rose from €10.6bn to €11.7bn at the end of December 2025.

It comes after S&P Global and Fitch, two of the world’s largest rating agencies, downgraded chunks of Ineos debt in recent months.

The latest results predate the outbreak of war in Iran, with bosses warning that the conflict risks piling more pressure on the debt-laden business.

An Ineos spokesman said: “While we do not have operations in the Middle East and revenue generated in this region is not material to the [Ineos] Group, the length, impact and outcome of the ongoing military conflict in the Middle East is highly unpredictable and there can be no assurances that further unforeseen events related to this conflict will not have a further material adverse affect on our operations in the future.”

The latest results emerged as Ineos warned investors of possible delays to its €4bn (£3.5bn) flagship petrochemicals plant because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Ineos Group told investors it was facing potential problems completing its Project One plant in Antwerp, Belgium, after two of its components were stranded in the Gulf.

The plant, which will make materials for plastic products and was set to be finished by the end of this year, was already suffering from delays after its environmental permit was suspended in 2023 by the Belgian government.

Ineos has described the €4bn Project One development as the largest investment in the European chemical sector in a generation. It said last June that the plant was 70pc completed.

Project One is made up of heavy modules which have been built across Thailand, the United Arab Emirates and the Philippines before being shipped to Antwerp.

Two of those shipments are stuck in a port in the Gulf.

Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz since Israel and the US first launched strikes on the country in late February.

Ineos said that rerouting tankers around the Cape of Good Hope, away from escalating tensions in the Middle East, had already cost the business more than €30m over the last two years.

S&P revealed its latest downgrade for Ineos’s debt last month.

It warned that Ineos was facing turmoil from “challenging trading conditions” around the world for the petrochemical sector. At one point, the company was reportedly considering asset sales as it looked to refinance some of its debt pile.

Sir Jim has blamed mounting losses at his Grangemouth chemical works on government carbon taxes and Britain’s sky-high energy costs.

However, he has also triggered controversy over his comments surrounding immigration.

The Manchester United co-owner, one of Britain’s richest men with a £17bn fortune, said earlier this year that Britain was being “colonised by immigrants”. He later apologised for “offending some people” with his language.

Ineos declined to comment.
