data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Next boss warns Government against profiteering from energy crisis
data.description:
The boss of Next has warned the Government against “profiting” from the Iran crisis as soaring oil and gas prices are expected to trigger higher tax revenues.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise said it was a “reasonable ask” for the Treasury not to retain any unexpected profits from disruption in the Middle East at a time when businesses are grappling with inflated costs.

His comments mark an escalation in tensions between the Government and the private sector after Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, blamed businesses for higher prices in the wake of the war.

The chief executive of Next said: “I think a reasonable ask from our industry – and in fact all industry – is that the Government doesn’t end up profiting from it.”

He singled out the prospect of higher revenues from fuel duty owing to increased petrol and diesel prices.

Lord Wolfson said it would be fair for the Treasury to take “what it was expecting from fuel but not necessarily more as a result of the price changes”.

“I think that would be a very reasonable ask for the industry to say that to the Government: ‘Don’t actually make more money out of this than you were expecting,’” he said.

Petrol forecourt bosses have hit back at claims they were profiteering from higher prices, arguing instead that the Treasury takes a bigger share of fuel sales than they did.

According to figures from the RAC, the retailer margin on a litre of petrol currently stands at 6pc.

Meanwhile, fuel duty comes in at 52.95p per litre. On top of that is VAT, charged at 20pc.

Petrol prices have risen almost 17p per litre since the start of the Iran conflict while diesel prices have risen around 33p per litre.

The Government is facing mounting pressure to help bring down prices for motorists.

Robert Jenrick, Reform’s economic spokesman, called for an emergency halving of VAT on road fuel on Thursday. He accused the Chancellor of “raking it in while motorists suffer”.

The Conservatives have urged the Government to scrap the upcoming fuel duty increase.

Previous analysis has suggested that the Treasury could also bank as much as £3bn more in tax revenues if the war in the Middle East results in higher oil and gas prices for longer.

Granville Park Partners said last week that it expected soaring energy costs to increase the corporation tax paid by North Sea firms by £1.5bn alone, while the windfall tax on energy companies’ profits would boost the Treasury’s coffers by a further £1.3bn if current oil and gas prices are sustained.

While rising prices at the pump won’t increase the fixed pence amount of fuel duty HMRC collects with every litre sold, the amount owed in VAT will.

While levied at 20pc, in line with most other goods and services, crucially, it is applied after fuel duty, constituting what many have called a “tax on a tax”. Higher petrol and diesel prices could deliver a windfall of £2.5bn to the Treasury.

Lord Wolfson said businesses should not be asking for tax cuts or reductions in levies to help them deal with extra costs, saying this would be “problematic”.

He weighed in on the debate after Next unveiled a £15m hit from the Middle East conflict, which could force it to push up prices as early as this summer.

The fashion retailer said the war in Iran was likely to have “knock-on effects” across its business, as it battles higher air freight fees and inflated fuel costs.

If the disruption drags on longer than expected, Next said it would have to start increasing prices in its stores as soon as June. Further price increases would come in the autumn.

Lord Wolfson said these would be “no more than 10pc”. However, it said this was currently a “contingency, not a plan”.

In its latest trading update on Thursday, the company said: “Much will depend on how long the conflict persists, and how much permanent damage is done to the world’s energy infrastructure.”

Next said if the war continued, extra costs would be reflected in “higher prices to consumers and disruption to our supply chain, both of which are likely to suppress sales”.

There are mounting fears that the war in Iran will start piling fresh pressure on shoppers as global trade chaos and higher energy costs threaten price rises.

Goldman Sachs has warned that inflation in Britain could hit 5pc this year “in a severely adverse scenario”. This compares to inflation of around 3pc last month, with households facing higher energy bills and grocery costs.

The Institute of Grocery Distribution has said food inflation could hit 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persisted.

However, Next’s warning suggests shoppers should brace for price rises not just in supermarkets, but across the high street.

Air cargo prices have already soared following strikes across the Middle East, as companies battle to avoid turmoil in key shipping lanes through the region. Rates rose 10pc this week, according to WorldACD Market Data, following an 8pc increase a week earlier. It also reflects sharp increases in fuel costs since the outbreak of war in Iran.

The price of Brent crude is up by more than a third since the conflict began, as Tehran halts oil flows out of the Gulf. Oil prices rose 2.5pc to nearly $105 in early trading on Thursday.

Next issued its sales warning as it said profits jumped 14.5pc to £1.2bn in the year to January. This was ahead of its earlier guidance, driven by better sales in January.

The retailer said sales had been “encouraging in the UK” since the end of its financial year, and had also been “strong overseas up to the point the conflict began in the Middle East”.

However, it said: “We have not yet reached the period of unusually strong UK trading we experienced last year and, perhaps more importantly, instability in the Middle East – which represents around 6pc of our total turnover – may continue to restrain growth in that region.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Making bombs won’t save Volkswagen
data.description:
It summons up dark memories. It may prove ethically troubling, both for its workforce and for its local community.

As reports emerge that Volkswagen may start making parts for the Israeli Iron Dome, lots of difficult questions are being asked about the pivot from making cars to manufacturing military equipment.

However, there is a more serious issue, both for VW and for the rest of Europe’s struggling auto industry.

What makes them think they will be any good at it?

Defence is a high-tech, fiercely competitive industry and not one that anyone can easily conquer.

VW certainly needs to try something. What was once the biggest auto company in the world and the emblem of Germany’s post-war economic miracle is in deep trouble.

Soaring energy costs in Germany have been a huge burden alongside the European Union’s blundering determination to impose a ban on new petrol and diesel cars in the 2030s.

Over the last five years, VW’s share price has fallen by more than 60pc and it has started to cut its workforce. Its net profits fell by 44pc last year. The company had planned to become a major manufacturer of electric vehicles but the new Chinese players appear to have that market sewn up with cheaper – and often better – models. The outlook is grim.

Its solution appears to be a pivot to the defence industry.

While VW has publicly ruled out making weapons, reports emerged this week that its Osnabrück plant, currently threatened with closure, may start making parts for the Iron Dome anti-missile system produced by Israel’s Rafael Advanced Defence Systems.

There were reports last year that Rheinmetall, Germany’s largest defence company, could take over the plant instead. We will see in the next few weeks what happens.

On one level, switching from cars to tanks and guns might make a lot of sense. Defence is booming, especially in Germany where the government is spending tens of billions of euros on re-equipping its armed forces. With the war in Iran, demand is only going to grow in the next few years.

Every government in the world will be looking at installing anti-missile shields – apart from the UK, where we will probably spend the money on extra welfare instead – as well as re-stocking arsenals that are already looking badly depleted.

Who knows, VW might set a template for the entire industry with the likes of BMW, Stellantis (the owner of Fiat and Citroën) and Renault all moving into defence manufacturing as well.

Even Britain’s Jaguar Land Rover could opt to go back to its roots – at least for its Land Rover unit – and start making military off-roaders instead of plush SUVs for the school run in the world’s more prosperous suburbs. It could be the saviour of an industry that looks as if it is heading into terminal trouble.

Here’s the problem, however. It will be a lot harder than it looks.

There may be some superficial similarities. An auto plant needs to bash a lot of metal, just as a defence factory does. It has to keep costs down and the lines rolling, and it needs to be able to put together a lot of complex parts very efficiently.

In a modest way, the auto manufacturers might be able to make a success of it, at least as subcontractors of the major established giants.

Even so, let’s be honest here. This is not the Second World War, when every available scrap of industrial capacity had to be converted to armaments manufacturing to keep the front lines supplied and every production site from bicycle factories to food plants suddenly found themselves making bullets and bombs.

Modern defence manufacturing involves a very different skill-set. It is high-precision and high-tech, and it involves vast quantities of research and development, as well as massive investments in a skilled workforce.

Just take a look at the world’s top defence manufacturers.

The top three are all American, led by Lockheed Martin, RTX and Northrop Grumman, with Britain’s BAE Systems coming in at number four and Italy’s Leonardo at number 11 in the global market.

These are all companies with long and deep histories in the industry, with established contracts and with relationships with the military that stretch back for generations. They spend billions of dollars developing the best kit that can be made and testing it in the most extreme conditions.

The idea that VW or any of the other traditional auto manufacturers can suddenly break into that list is laughable. It is not going to happen. They don’t have the skills or the expertise, nor are the production lines set up in the right way.

Even if they were, it is a fiercely competitive industry, with their customers largely national governments who are going to spend their budgets on domestic manufacturers instead of in other countries.

If there is space for new players, they are far more likely to be from Ukraine – where local manufacturers have been forced to become world leaders in cheap, effective drones – or from Israel – where they have been forced to become experts in missile protection – than from Germany, France or indeed Britain. It isn’t going to happen.

Europe’s traditional auto giants certainly need to do something. With each month that passes, it becomes increasingly clear that attempts to replace petrol cars with battery-powered ones has been catastrophically mismanaged.

Bureaucrats and politicians appear to care more about grandstanding to the green lobby than preserving vital jobs and manufacturing capacity. It is hard to see how any of them are going to survive the Chinese onslaught on the industry.

Even so, these companies are kidding themselves if they think that switching to making bullets and bombs will save them.

It won’t. All it will do is guarantee terminal decline.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ex-BP boss sunk by workplace affair hired by CIA-backed tech company
data.description:
The former boss of BP has been recruited to run a CIA-backed tech company in his first executive role since being ousted over a workplace fling.

Bernard Looney, 56, was hired by AI company Prometheus Hyperscale as its chief executive on Wednesday. The group, backed by the US intelligence service’s venture capital arm In-Q-Tel, is seeking to build “zero-carbon” facilities in Wyoming and Texas.

It marks a return to front-line duties for the former oilman, who resigned from BP three years ago after failing to properly declare past romantic relationships with his colleagues.

At the time, BP said he “knowingly misled” the board over the relationships. He was forced to forfeit £32m of pay awards because of the scandal.

Since then, the oil company has struggled for focus with Mr Looney’s successor Murray Auchincloss stepping down after just two years in charge.

He has been replaced by Meg O’Neill, who took up her role on Wednesday, promising “more consistency” from the company.

While at BP, Mr Looney was a well-known advocate for switching to green energy and cutting back on its legacy business of oil and gas production – plans Mr Auchincloss and now Ms O’Neill are seeking to unwind.

That green energy knowledge will be handy at Prometheus, which is seeking to spend $30bn (£22.5bn) on two data hubs in the US using supplies of natural gas, nuclear power and carbon capture technologies to cut its emissions.

The plants, which will provide 2.5 gigawatts of computing capacity, will also use geothermal cooling technology to reduce their water intensity. Mr Looney has been chairman of the group since 2024.

His appointment comes amid an AI infrastructure building boom that has seen hundreds of billions directed towards data centres, with the intent to power chatbot apps such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

Data centre developers have been seeking to tap new power sources to run racks of thousands of processors. They have also been building their own power supplies, resulting from constraints on power grids.

Prometheus has said its data centre developments are intended to be “zero-carbon” by using carbon capture technologies and by purchasing carbon removal credits.

Other Silicon Valley giants have turned to fossil fuels including gas turbines to power their data projects, despite the potential effect on their net zero targets.

Prometheus’s Wyoming site will use a combination of gas and nuclear reactors developed by Oklo, a start-up previously chaired by Sam Altman, the OpenAI chief.

The former BP boss replaces Trenton Thornock, Prometheus’s founder, as chief executive. In-Q-Tel, which makes strategic investments to boost the CIA, is one of its major backers.

Mr Looney said: “As artificial intelligence and digital technologies continue to reshape our world, it is crucial that we build the necessary infrastructure responsibly. This is the mission we have set ourselves.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ryanair chief warns of summer flight cancellations
data.description:
The boss of Ryanair has warned of flight cancellations this summer as the war in Iran sparks fears of jet fuel shortages.

Michael O’Leary said the UK was more exposed to the crisis than any other European country because of its heavy reliance on fuel from the Middle East.

He said: “We can handle, we think, the pricing issue, but if there’s a risk to 10 or 20pc of the fuel supply in June or July or August, then we and all other airlines will have to start looking at cancelling some flights or taking some capacity out.”

Mr O’Leary said he had been given assurances that fuel supplies would be stable until mid-to-late May, but that they could start to dry up if the war in the Middle East drags into the summer.

He said: “If this continues through to the end of April, we’re looking at a risk to supplies in early June. If it runs into May, then we don’t know what the f- - - goes on.”

Mr O’Leary said he was holding daily calls with his fuel team and that Ryanair was reviewing its schedule on a weekly basis.

It comes as the airline industry grapples with the fallout from the war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial choke point through which a fifth of the world’s oil usually passes.

The crisis has caused jet fuel prices to more than double, with concerns now growing of a shortage of the fuel, which uses more oil in the refining process than petrol or diesel.

Thousands of flights have already been cancelled as a result of the crisis, compounding disruption caused by the closure of key travel hubs in the Middle East.

Woo Kee-hong, chief executive of Korean Air, this week told staff the airline was in “emergency mode”. German carrier Lufthansa has also drawn up crisis plans that include potentially grounding up to 40 aircraft, or 5pc of its total capacity.

The EU has urged people to cut back on flying and driving to help save fuel and urged governments to consider emergency measures. By contrast, ministers in the UK have insisted that supplies are plentiful and have told people to go about their daily lives as normal.

Mr O’Leary dismissed the idea of fuel rationing but warned that the UK’s airline industry was more exposed to potential shortages as it sourced 25pc of its jet fuel supplies from Kuwait.

He said: “Of all the European countries at the moment, the one that is most vulnerable is the UK, because [of] the market share that the Kuwaitis have here in the UK.”

Only a handful of cargoes of fuel from the Middle East are heading to Britain, with the UK’s last known shipment of jet fuel from Saudi Arabia expected to arrive tomorrow.

Ryanair is planning to launch five new routes in and out of London, including to the Italian cities of Forli and Parma, as well as to Glasgow, Malmö and Wroclaw in Poland. The cut-price airline has increased its passenger target for the upcoming year to 216 million, an increase of eight million on last year.

However, Mr O’Leary admitted that his airline would not have the luxury of choosing which flights to cut should there be fuel shortages. Instead, he said it would have to respond to supply disruptions at specific airports.

Ryanair was forced to cancel its 12 daily flights to Jordan following the outbreak of the conflict.

However, it said it had benefited from families cancelling Easter trips to the Middle East or long-haul trips through the Gulf and booking European holidays with Ryanair instead. As a result, bookings are up around 5pc over the Easter period.
